diagrams.title: Real-World Applications of Regular Expressions
diagrams.description: A comprehensive diagram showing various domains where regular expressions are applied in computing
elements: Central node: Regular Expressions Applications
Branch 1: Text Processing (Text editors, Search/Replace, Data extraction)
Branch 2: Programming Tools (Lexical Analyzers, Tokenizers, Parsers)
Branch 3: Validation (Form validation, Email/URL/Phone validation, Input sanitization)
Branch 4: Network Security (Firewall patterns, Intrusion detection, Log analysis)
Branch 5: Data Science (Web scraping, Log parsing, Data cleaning)
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the breadth of regex applications beyond theoretical context, seeing how the same concept applies across software development domains

diagrams.title: Lexical Analyzer Architecture
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing how regular expressions are used in compiler design for lexical analysis
elements: Source Code Input (left arrow)
Lexical Analyzer Component (large rectangle)
Internal: Regex Patterns → NFA → DFA → Token Stream
Output: Token Stream to Parser (right arrow)
Symbol Table connection (downward arrow)
Legend showing the conversion flow: Pattern Specification → Finite Automaton → Token Generation
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the practical application of regex in compiler construction, showing how theoretical automata concepts translate to real compiler components

diagrams.title: Regex Pattern Matching Process Flow
diagrams.description: Flowchart depicting how a regex engine processes input string against a pattern
elements: Start node: Input String + Regex Pattern
Decision diamond: Pattern syntax valid?
Process box: Compile regex to NFA/DFA
Process box: Execute finite automaton on input
Decision diamond: Match found?
Output nodes: Matched substring / No match / Partial match
Arrows showing backtracking for greedy vs lazy matching
diagrams.purpose: Shows students how the abstract regex notation is actually processed by computers, connecting theory to implementation

diagrams.title: Common Regex Patterns in Software Development
diagrams.description: Visual catalog of frequently used regex patterns with examples and use cases
elements: Category: Email Validation - Pattern: ^[\w.-]+@[\w.-]+\.\w+$ - Example: user@example.com
Category: Phone Numbers - Pattern: ^\+?[1-9]\d{1,14}$ - Example: +14155551234
Category: Password Strength - Pattern: ^(?=.*[a-z])(?=.*[A-Z])(?=.*\d)(?=.*[@$!%*?&])[A-Za-z\d@$!%*?&]{8,}$
diagrams.purpose: Provides practical reference for students to understand how regex solves everyday programming problems

diagrams.title: Regex Metacharacters Reference Card
diagrams.description: Visual grid showing all regex metacharacters grouped by function with examples
elements: Group 1: Anchors (^ start, $ end, \b word boundary)
Group 2: Quantifiers (* zero+, + one+, ? optional, {n} exact, {n,m} range)
Group 3: Character Classes ([abc] any, [^abc] not, [a-z] range, . any char)
Group 4: Groups ((abc) capture, (?:abc) non-capture, (?=abc) lookahead)
Group 5: Alternation (| or)
Group 6: Escape sequences (\d digit, \w word char, \s whitespace)
Color coding: Green for basic, Yellow for intermediate, Red for advanced
diagrams.purpose: Serves as quick reference for students when writing and debugging regex patterns

charts.title: Regex Flavors Comparison Table
charts.content: Comparison of Basic Regular Expressions (BRE), Extended Regular Expressions (ERE), and Perl-Compatible Regular Expressions (PCRE): BRE supports basic metacharacters with escaping for |, (, ), {, +; ERE adds direct support for |, (, ), +, ?, {, }; PCRE adds lookahead, lookbehind, named groups, recursive patterns. POSIX ERE is standard in Unix tools like grep -E, sed -E. JavaScript regex supports lookahead but not lookbehind. Python re module follows PCRE with some differences.

charts.title: Regex vs Finite Automata vs Regular Grammar
charts.content: Three equivalent representations of regular languages: Regular Expressions use algebraic notation with *, |, concatenation; Finite Automata use state diagrams with transitions (DFA deterministic, NFA nondeterministic); Regular Grammar uses production rules (left-linear or right-linear). All three can represent exactly the same set of languages (regular languages). Conversions exist between all pairs: Regex→NFA (Thompson's construction), NFA→DFA (subset construction), DFA→Regex (state elimination).

charts.title: Pattern Matching Decision Tree
charts.content: Flowchart for choosing regex approach: Start → Is pattern simple literal? → Yes → Use strchr/find → No → Does pattern need alternation? → Yes → Use regex with | operator → No → Does pattern need repetition? → Yes → Use * or + quantifiers → No → Does pattern need character classes? → Yes → Use [] notation → No → Use basic concatenation. Shows how to systematically build complex patterns from basic operations.

charts.title: Greedy vs Lazy Quantifiers
charts.content: Comparison showing the difference in matching behavior: Greedy quantifiers (*, +, {n,}) match as much as possible and backtrack if needed; Lazy quantifiers (*?, +?, {n,}?) match as few as possible and expand if needed. Example: Given 'bold' with pattern '<.+>' gives greedy match 'bold' while '<.+?>' gives '' and '' separately. Performance implications: greedy can be slower on complex patterns, lazy often more intuitive.

branches: Theoretical Foundation: Regular Languages, Equivalence with FA, Regular Grammars
Language Processing: Lexical Analysis, Token Generation, Pattern Recognition
Software Development: Input Validation, Search & Replace, String Manipulation
System Administration: Log Parsing, Configuration Files, Log Monitoring
Network & Security: Firewall Rules, Intrusion Detection, Data Extraction
Data Analysis: Web Scraping, CSV Parsing, Data Validation & Cleaning
Tools & Implementations: grep, sed, awk, Perl, Python re, JavaScript regex
Advanced Features: Lookahead/Lookbehind, Named Groups, Backreferences, Anchors
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting theoretical concepts to practical applications across computing disciplines

branches: Basic Operations: Union (|), Concatenation (implicit), Kleene Star (*)
Literals & Metacharacters: Literal characters match self, Metacharacters (. ^ $ * + ? [ ] \ ( ) { } |) need escaping
Quantifiers: Exact {n}, Range {n,m}, Zero+ *, One+ ?, Optional ?
Assertions: Lookahead (?=), Negative lookahead (?!), Lookbehind (?<=), Negative lookbehind (?<!)
Practical Patterns: Email, URL, Phone, IP, Date, Credit Card, Password Validation
mindMaps.purpose: Structured reference for students to understand regex syntax hierarchy from basic to advanced features

visuals.description: Visual diagram for applications of regular expressions
